title: Mycobacterium tuberculosis DNA Extraction Using InstaGene Matrix and High Speed Homogenizer
author: Emilyn C Conceição

steps.instruction: Perform quality control of the genomic DNA to check the quantity and purity. The DNA quantity should measure the double strand (ds) DNA using the high-sensitivity (HS) Qubit kit (following the manufacturer's instructions).

We acknowledge the TORCH consortium team for their valuable contributions to the development of this protocol. We thank MSc. Janré Steyn for providing user-based feedback. We are grateful to the insightful revisions offered by Dr. Anzaan Dippenaar and Dr. Fahd Naufal.
IF WORKING WITH SOLID CULTURE This workflow is visually represented in Figure 1 (if using solid culture). Figure 1. Workflow to process solid culture for DNA extraction.

CTAB: Cetyltrimethylammonium-bromideCPC: clinical primary cultureddH 2 O: Double distilled waterBMRI: Biomedical Research InstituteBSC: Biological Safety CabinetBSL3: Biosafety level 3 laboratoryCaCl 2 : Calcium ChlorideDNA: deoxyribonucleic acid FP: Fast Prep HSH: High-Speed Homogenizer IGM: InstaGene MatrixLJ: Löwenstein–JensenMgCl 2 : Magnesium Chloride SolutionMGIT: mycobacteria growth indicator tubeMTBC: Mycobacterium tuberculosis complexNFW: Nuclease-free waterNGS: Next-generation sequencing PANTA: Polymyxin B, Amphotericin B, Nalidixic Acid, Trimethoprim, Azlocillin PAPR: powered air-purifying respiratorPPE: personal protective equipmenttNGS: target next-generation sequencingTris-HCL: Tris-hydrochloride QC: Quality ControlRCF: Relative centrifugal force SOP: Standard Operating ProcedureWGS: Whole-genome sequencing
Label the 1.5 or 2 mL tubes (containing 3 sterile beads of 2 mm) with the corresponding sample identification on the culture tubes.
Add 200 µL of nuclease-free water (NFW) to the labelled tubes. This step can be done in a BSL2 laboratory.
In a laminar flow hood (Level II), scrape 1 loop size of 10 μL of mycobacterial culture into the labelled tubes.
Vortex the tubes until the clumped cells are dispersed.
Heat-inactivate the bacterial samples at 80 °C for 01:00:00 before being removed from the BSL3.
Place the aliquoted culture (5 mL) into the pre-heated oven at 80 °C and allow it to inactivate for 01:00:00 at 80 °C before being removed from the BSL3 laboratory.
Wipe the outer surface of each tube and transfer the heat-inactivated liquid culture samples to a BSL2 laboratory.
Transfer 5 mL of the BBL MGIT content (liquid culture) into 15 mL a polypropylene tube using a 2 or 3 mL Pasteur pipette and discard the pipette.

Vortex the MGIT tube (liquid culture in 7H9 MGIT media) previously confirmed as positive for M. tuberculosis until the bacterial biomass from the bottom is homogenized and the clumped cells are dispersed (about 00:00:05 ).
Label each 15 mL polypropylene tube with the sample identification.
IF WORKING WITH LIQUID CULTURE (MGIT) This workflow is visually represented in Figure 2 (if using liquid culture). Figure 2. Workflow to process liquid culture for DNA extraction.
Wipe the outer surface of the tube and remove the heat-inactivated solid culture samples from the BSL3 to a BSL2 laboratory.

This workflow is visually represented in Figure 3 (if using liquid culture). Figure 3. Pre-treatment workflow for DNA extraction from heat-inactivated liquid culture. When dealing with CPC samples, host (human) DNA and other contaminants persist (bacterial, fungi and viral), even after sputum decontamination and using the BD MGIT PANTA antibiotic mixture within the BD MGIT BBL tube. To address this issue, it is advised to use a saline wash as a pre-treatment step in conjunction with DNase I enzyme digestion to remove contaminating extracellular DNA. This combined approach eliminates non-mycobacterial DNA that can interfere with subsequent WGS processes. In the context of tNGS, a saline wash as pre-treatment is not required because non-mycobacterial DNA does not amplify and thereby affects the analysis, given that the NGS is a targeted PCR approach. Figure 2. Pre-treatment workflow for DNA extraction from heat-inactivated solid culture.
In the general BSL2 lab, centrifuge the 15 mL tubes at 4.000 x g, , for 00:30:00 at Room temperature .
Using a Pasteur pipette carefully aspirate the supernatant, without disturbing the pellet, and discard it.
Resuspend the pellet by adding 5 mL of 1X DNase buffer.
Homogenize using a vortex for 00:00:10 to disperse cells.
Centrifuge the 15 mL tubes at 4.000 x g, , for 00:30:00 at Room temperature .
Using a Pasteur pipette carefully aspirate the supernatant, without disturbing the pellet, and discard it.
Remove the remaining supernatant with a P1000 pipette.

Resuspend the pellet in 500 µL of 1X DNase buffer within the 15 mL tube. To better obtain the mycobacterial cells: Resuspend the pellet in 350 µL of 1x DNase buffer and mix gently. Transfer to a 1.5 or 2 mL low-binding screw cap tube. Add 150 µL of 1x DNase buffer to the remaining pellet within the 15 mL tube. Pipette up and down to clean the tips/tube (collect as many cells as possible). Transfer the cells to the same low-binding screw cap tube to have a final volume of 500 µL .
Add 10 µL (15 U/µL, totalling 150 U per sample) of DNase I enzyme.
Incubate at 37 °C for 00:30:00 with gentle shaking to digest extracellular DNA (host and commensal) in suspension from the host or commensal organisms.
Incubate at 65 °C for 00:10:00 to deactivate the DNase I enzyme.
Centrifuge at 21000 rcf, , (full speed) for 00:20:00 to pellet the mycobacterial cells.
Discard the supernatant carefully using a P200 or P100 pipette without disturbing the pellet.
Proceed with IGM/FP DNA extraction.

Before the DNA extraction (Illustrated in Figure 4), proceed as follows: Prepare a batch of tubes as follows: Add three 2 mm beads to each 1.5 or 2 mL low-binding screw-cap tube and autoclave. Remove the IGM from refrigeration ( 4 °C ) and allow it to thaw to equilibrate Room temperature at least 00:30:00 . Subsequently, homogenize the IGM using a magnetic mixer according to the manufacturer's guidelines. Figure 4. Schematic representation of DNA extraction using InstaGene TM matrix kit with a high-speed homogenizer (e.g.: FastPrep). A) Steps if using liquid culture (from 1-A to 4A); B) Steps if using solid culture (from 1-B to 4B); C) Common procedures for both types of culture.

In the general BSL2 lab, centrifuge the 15 mL tubes at rpm, °C, 4.000 x g (if using liquid culture) for a duration of 00:30:00 or at 12000 x g, °C, (if using solid culture) or at Room temperature Skip this step if the pre-treatment of the liquid heat-inactivated culture was performed.
Gently resuspend the cell pellet with a P200 pipette and transfer the entire suspension to the pre-labelled tube containing the three beads. Recover all possible cells from the tube wall.

Add 200 µL of well-mixed IGM to the 15 mL tube containing the pellet of the heat-inactivated culture. If using solid culture, centrifuge the 1.5 or 2 mL tubes with the samples at 12.000 rcf, , for 00:01:00 and remove the supernatant without resuspending the pellet.
Incubate at 56 °C for 00:15:00 .
Homogenize using a vortex for 00:00:10 to disperse cells.
Incubate the tubes in a heating block set at 100 °C for 00:08:00 .
Homogenize the samples using an HSH FastPrep or BeadBlaster 24 (program: 00:01:00 at 4.0 m/s, 1 cycle).
Centrifuge the tubes at 12.000 rcf, , for 00:15:00 .
Transfer 130 µL of the supernatant with DNA to a 1.5 or 2 mL tube without disturbing the pellet (matrix with cell debris).
Proceed with quality control (QC).

If samples exhibit high levels of salt or protein contamination, DNA purification can be performed using AMPure XP beads (Figure 5). Figure 5. Workflow to process genomic DNA purification using AMPure beads.

Prepare cultured samples, reagents, and review the equipment and consumable list before beginning the DNA extraction process. The method includes the following five sections: 1) Mycobacterial culture preparation and heat-inactivation (In the BSL3). 2) Pre-treatment of the heat-inactivated culture before DNA extraction. 3) DNA extraction using InstaGene matrix and bead-beating. 4) DNA quality control. 5) Purification with AMPure XP Bead-Based Reagent (Beckman Coulter) (Optional for WGS).

The DNA purity can be measured using the Nanodrop spectrophotometer (following the manufacturer's instructions). For the DNA concentration, the 260/230 and 260/280 ratios are essential indicators. The reference values are for DNA samples: for the 260/280 ratio, it should be 1.8. Values between 1.7 and 2.0 indicate some degree of contamination and may influence reactions depending on the downstream procedure. A minimum DNA concentration of 1.0 ng/µL is required for sequencing with the Illumina platform. The 260/230 ratio should be between 1.8 and 2.2. In general , if the ratio is remarkably different than the provided range, it may indicate the presence of residual phenol, guanidine, magnetic beads, carbohydrates, or proteins. A low 260/230 ratio may indicate an issue with the buffer used for the blank measurement.

In general , if the ratio is remarkably different than the provided range, it may indicate the presence of residual phenol, guanidine, magnetic beads, carbohydrates, or proteins. A low 260/230 ratio may indicate an issue with the buffer used for the blank measurement.

steps.instruction: Subsequently, aliquot the desired amount into a low-binding tube(s) based on the number of samples being purified. Allow the aliquot to thaw to Room temperature for 00:30:00 before use.
Incubate for 00:00:30 and remove by aspiration the 80% ethanol.

Based on the sample volume, add a 1.2 X volume ratio of AMPure XP beads to the 1.5 mL or 2 mL low-binding tube containing the DNA sample. For example: If only 50 µL of the DNA sample is purified, use 60 µL of AMPure XP beads to get a ratio of 1.2x (AMPure XP beads to DNA sample volume).
Homogenize by pipetting up and down 10 X (times) slowly to avoid creating bubbles. If bubbles are introduced, this will reduce the final volume of the purified DNA.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place the tube into the magnetic rack for 00:05:00 until the supernatant is clear.

With the tube on the magnetic rack, carefully remove the supernatant without resuspending or touching the beads. Avoid touching the beads while aspirating the liquid. Instead, position the pipette tip on the opposite side of the beads inside the tube.

Add 100 µL of freshly prepared 80% ethanol on the opposite side of the beads, while the tubes are still on the magnetic rack. 1. Do not add the 80% ethanol directly onto the beads. 2. When cleaning up larger volumes of DNA and beads, increase the drying time. A standard 00:05:00 drying time is sufficient for cleaning up 50 µL of DNA using 100 µL of ethanol. For larger DNA volumes, proportionally increase the ethanol volume. For example, when cleaning up 130 µL of DNA using 156 µL of beads, use 200 µL of ethanol.
go to step #1 Repeat steps 41 and 42. Use a P10 pipette to remove any residual 80% ethanol at the bottom of the tube on after the second wash.
After the drying step, remove the tubes from the magnetic rack.
Immediately add 50 µL of NFW and resuspend the beads to release the DNA in the NFW. To obtain a more concentrated DNA sample, use 25 µL of NFW instead of 50 µL .
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place the tubes back on the magnetic rack and incubate for 00:05:00 .
Transfer the supernatant (containing eluted DNA) of each sample to a newly labelled tube.
Proceed with DNA QC.

Allow the 80% ethanol to evaporate without allowing the beads to crack (for about 00:05:00 ). 1. After the removal of the residual 80% ethanol, the beads have a shiny finish. When the 80% ethanol has evaporated, the beads will have a matte finish and immediately resuspend the beads in NFW to prevent over-drying and cracking. When more than 2 tubes have matte beads, close the tubes and add NFW to each tube and resuspend. This will also prevent over-drying of beads when working with a larger batch of samples. 2. When processing increased amounts of DNA and beads, double the standard drying time (about 00:10:00 ).
Begin by thoroughly vortexing the cold ( 4 °C ) AMPure XP beads to resuspend the magnetic beads that may have settled.

In a BSL3 facility, prepare the CPC Mycobacterial culture sample by performing the quality control (QC) using the rapid immunochromatographic assay (antigen test, Capilia‱ TB-Neo assay) for MTBC detection.
